sections.heading: Consolidation of Power, 1933–1934
sections.text: After becoming Chancellor, Hitler moved rapidly to dismantle Weimar democracy. The Reichstag Fire Decree (28 Feb 1933) suspended civil liberties; the Enabling Act (23 March 1933) gave the cabinet legislative power; trade unions were dissolved (May 1933); all other parties were banned (July 1933). The Night of the Long Knives (30 June 1934) eliminated the SA leadership. When Hindenburg died (2 August 1934), Hitler merged the offices of Chancellor and President.

sections.heading: Economic Policy and Rearmament
sections.text: Hitler appointed Hjalmar Schacht as Economics Minister. A massive public works and rearmament programme reduced unemployment from 6 million to 300,000 by 1938 — though achieved through deficit spending and autarky that made Germany economically dependent on continued expansion. Rearmament violated the Versailles Treaty openly from 1935.

sections.heading: Foreign Policy and Expansion, 1936–1939
sections.text: A series of diplomatic coups — Rhineland remilitarisation (1936), Anschluss with Austria (1938), annexation of the Sudetenland (Munich Agreement, 1938), occupation of Czechoslovakia (1939) — were achieved without war. Hitler correctly judged that France and Britain would not fight. The invasion of Poland in September 1939 finally triggered the war he had long sought but on a timetable and with enemies he had not intended.

sections.heading: Wartime Leadership, 1939–1945
sections.text: Initially brilliant — France fell in six weeks, most of Europe was conquered — Hitler's strategic intuition increasingly failed from 1941. His decision to invade the Soviet Union, declare war on the United States, interfere personally in military decisions, and refuse strategic withdrawals led to a series of catastrophes: Stalingrad, the loss of North Africa, Normandy, and the encirclement of Germany on all fronts. He remained in the Berlin bunker as Soviet forces entered the city, marrying Eva Braun on 29 April 1945 and dictating a final testament blaming the Jews for the war before shooting himself on 30 April.
